policy_statement: Benchmark 1.3 billion square feet of communitywide buildings by 2026.
mechanism_description: Benchmarking and disclosing building energy use creates transparency and comparative signals that motivate or enable energy-efficiency improvements, reducing emissions over time.
typology_evidence_quote: "Benchmark 1.3 billion square feet of communitywide buildings by 2026."
primary_causal_pathway: Information transparency (benchmarking/disclosure) → market/reputational/regulatory pressure → building efficiency upgrades & improved operations → ↓ energy demand → ↓ fossil generation/combustion → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Building owners/operators (and sometimes tenants, lenders, and buyers) measure, report, and disclose standardized energy performance (e.g., kWh/ft², ENERGY STAR score), enabling comparisons across buildings and time.  
2) Mediating physical/process mechanism: The information signal triggers actions such as commissioning, retrofits (HVAC, controls, envelope, lighting), and operational improvements; these actions reduce the physical energy required to deliver the same building services (heating/cooling/lighting/plug loads).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower electricity and onsite fuel consumption reduces CO₂ (and associated upstream) emissions from power plants and combustion equipment, yielding net GHG reductions over time.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is that transparency-driven efficiency actions reduce building energy consumption, thereby lowering GHG emissions from electricity generation and onsite fuel use.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Information disclosure induces efficiency improvements that reduce energy demand and thus reduce GHG emissions—this is a mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although the mechanism is “information/market signal,” the principal climate outcome is not merely resource efficiency in the abstract; it is specifically reduced fossil energy consumption leading to lower emissions (Mitigation).  
3) Edge cases applied: If benchmarking is used only for internal tracking with no follow-on actions, emissions impacts may be weaker; however, the mechanism as defined (“market signal for efficiency”) presumes behavior/investment response toward efficiency, keeping mitigation dominant.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that benchmarks/discloses building energy performance to motivate efficiency improvements shares the same primary emissions-reduction pathway regardless of jurisdiction or building segment.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether a specific policy functions mainly through voluntary action, compliance pressure, linkage to incentives, or as a precursor to performance standards is policy-specific and should be assigned based on policy-level evidence, not at this mechanism-level classification.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: In grids with very low carbon intensity, the emissions benefit per kWh saved is smaller (still mitigation). If disclosure is framed primarily as water benchmarking or broader sustainability reporting, non-energy outcomes may dominate; but for this canonical mechanism focused on energy efficiency signals, mitigation remains primary.
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: lower energy bills, improved indoor comfort, reduced peak demand/heat stress impacts
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as an information-disclosure/benchmarking requirement if the broader policy specifies mandatory reporting; however, as written it is framed as a benchmark coverage goal (“benchmark X sqft by Y date”), which fits target-setting more cleanly than a clear disclosure mandate.
